Re:Learning JS From Zero — Day 2: Variables, Your Must-Pick Starter Skill

Day 2 of being reincarnated into this new world. Before we do anything fancy, we need to learn how to create a variable. Variables are something you'll use constantly when building systems, so think of it as a must-pick starter skill on your tech tree.

So how do you assign a value to a variable? Assignment uses an operator — the equals (assignment) operator (more on that later). It looks like this ↓
assigning a value to a variable
Here's the thing: if you don't assign a value, JavaScript hands the variable a default keyword —
. During assignment, JavaScript takes the value on the right and stores it into the variable name on the left.
When you assign a value to a variable, there are three basic (primitive) types you'll usually run into:
String — anything wrapped in single or double quotes is a string.
The string type is the one exception: when you assign a string value, you absolutely must wrap it in single quotes (

Generally there are a few key points to watch when naming variables. Stick to these and you'll cut down on your chances of
Don't use keywords or reserved words as variable names, like
If the value is a string, remember to wrap it in single or double quotes.
Be consistent with upper/lowercase in variable names.

Remember to wrap strings in single or double quotes
Devs who've been at it a while usually pair this with a style guide to keep their JavaScript consistent. So for plain strings, I'd suggest using single quotes:
Double quotes work fine too, of course. I just personally recommend getting used to single quotes early, so that when you start dealing with style tooling later it doesn't feel like such a pain.
If a variable is originally called
when you call it, you'll definitely get an error ↓
So the casing of a variable has to be clear and consistent.
Variable names must start with a letter or $, never a number
You can start a variable name with an underscore or a
sign, but you just can't use a number, or special characters like
SyntaxError: identifier starts immediately after numeric literal
Don't assign undefined to a variable
By default, JavaScript sets up a space for your variable and assigns it a default keyword —
. In real-world dev, the moment we see
our gut reaction is "oh! this variable hasn't been assigned a value yet!" So if you go and assign
to a variable yourself, it becomes really hard to tell whether you actually gave it a value or not. And since assigning
doesn't throw any error, doing this leaves you with no idea where the problem is.

Last thing — let me fill in some of the terms I mentioned earlier, namely keywords. Keywords are also called reserved words, and what they mean is: stuff that already exists the moment the programming language is created.

Here's an analogy. Say you want to cast a spell in this other world — you have to chant an incantation, and the incantation must start with the three words "Come forth!" So when you summon Zenki, it's "Come forth! Zenki!" (huh?!). Those three words "Come forth!" are the reserved/keyword part. You can't go using them inside your spell like "Come forth! Come forth!" —
because then nobody would have a clue what you're trying to do
The next post, Re:Learning JS From Zero — Day 3, will cover selectors.

Day 2 in our isekai. Before anything else, we need to learn how to create a variable — it's something you'll reach for constantly when building systems. Think of it as a must-pick starter skill on your tech tree. This post covers what variables are, how to declare them, the three basic types, and the naming rules you really don't want to break.
